text:
In this reflective retrospective, Robin D.G. Kelley revisits his early scholarly engagement with the South African liberation movement, specifically his 1986 *Ufahamu* article on the Comintern and the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA). Writing from the vantage point of three decades later, Kelley evaluates the "Native Republic" thesis—the 1928 Comintern directive that called for an independent indigenous state as a necessary stage toward socialism. He argues that this policy was not a foreign imposition but a reflection of how African and Coloured nationalists radicalized the CPSA from within, transforming a white-led organization into a vehicle for Black self-determination and the reclaiming of land.

Beyond historical analysis, the essay connects the intellectual environment of UCLA’s anti-apartheid activism in the 1980s to the contemporary political landscape of post-1994 South Africa. Kelley addresses the paradox of the South African Communist Party’s (SACP) continued alliance with an ANC government that has embraced neoliberal reforms. By examining the rise of the Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) and their echoes of early 20th-century radicalism, the author questions whether the dream of economic liberation remains unfulfilled. Ultimately, the piece serves as both a personal intellectual history and a critical meditation on the tensions between national liberation, global capital, and the enduring quest for a "workers' and peasants' republic."
